text:
Equipment: RIE

RIE Quiz Question Finalization:

This document is broken down into each of the 8 sections of the RIE module. Each has its own page.

Question options are a combination of two documents, one I have previously shared, and one that Mohammed had provided in Teams. I have refactored all of his questions into a multiple-choice format found mixed in here. I have also removed a couple of my own questions that I felt were not at the same level of rigor/applicability as his questions.

Items To Be Addressed:

Underneath each of these items to be addressed, feel free to write:

[Your Name: Your thoughts, with references to a proposed quiz question which is denoted by Section (S)#, Question (Q)#]

Are 3 quiz questions per section too much? If so, please indicate which question you think should be eliminated from each section besides section 4.

Olivia: I believe photolithography currently has 1-2 questions per section. All of the sections of this document have 3 except for section 4 (Loading the Wafer) which only has 2. I personally think 3 would be okay, but I would like to hear other opinions.

Equipment: RIE

There is a lack of low-rigor questions similar to the photolithography module where the user matches equipment names to their picture. Do we feel like we need some of these for this module?

Olivia: I am unsure how helpful those style questions were for the photolithography module since there is not very much equipment in that room. While I do think they would be more useful for this module since there is more equipment, I must then question our consistency in quiz difficulty across the modules if we elect to not include that question type in RIE. Should we not then add more conceptual, process-centric questions to the photolithography module?

I am planning on presenting these questions in the order that they are numbered in this document. Please indicate if you think the order should be adjusted in any way.

Any other comments/questions/concerns about the proposed quiz content?

SECTION 1) Support Room Machine Startup

S1Q1: What is a Reactive Ion Etching (RIE) system used for in microfabrication?

To add material to a substrate using a liquid chemical bath.

To remove material using a chemically reactive plasma in a vacuum chamber.

Equipment: RIE

To physically remove material with high-energy ions, but without chemical reactions.

To clean silicon wafers using a solution of hydrofluoric acid.

Answer: B. RIE uses chemically reactive plasma within a vacuum environment to remove material from a substrate. It is a type of dry etching system.

S1Q2: Which of the following materials cannot be etched with RIE?

Polymers

Si

SiO2

Au

Si3N

GaAs

Answer: D. Au or Gold is chemically inert and doesn't form volatile compounds easily with common RIE gases (fluorine, chlorine, oxygen plasmas). It is typically removed by wet etching, not RIE.

S1Q3: T/F: To turn on the gas tanks, you rotate the valves in the clockwise direction.

Answer: False. The statement is backwards. You rotate the valves counterclockwise.

SECTION 2) Front Room Machine Startup

S2Q1: Which of the following is NOT a part of the safety precautions for operating an RIE system?

Use appropriate PPE (gloves, goggles, lab coat)

Confirm gas line integrity and exhaust function before starting

Understand the properties and hazards of process gases and chemicals before operation

Modifying procedures as needed without authorization

Equipment: RIE

Understand and be familiar with emergency shutdown procedures

Answer: D

S2Q2: Which pair of etch gases and materials is unsuitable?

Si: SF6 or CF4

SiO2: CHF3 or CF4/O2

Si3N4: CHF3/O2

Metals (e.g.,Al): CL2/BCl3

Quartz: Cl2/BCl3

Photoresist: O2

Answer: E. Quartz is SiO2, which has a suitable pairing with CHF3 or CF4/O2

S2Q3: What temperature should the front-room chiller reach before you start using the computer to run the RIE machine?

20 C

21 C

22 C

23 C

24 C

Answer: A. 20 C

SECTION 3) Pre-Load Software Steps

S3Q1: When reading the screen of the RIE, what does ATP and ACT refer to?

ATP: Wafer Temperature, ACT: Chamber Temperature

ATP: Machine Controller Temperature, ACT: Turbo Pump Temperature

ATP: Machine Stage Temperature, ACT: Plasma Temperature

ATP: Turbo Pump Temperature, ACT: Machine Controller Temperature

Answer: D. ATP: Turbo Pump Temperature, ACT: Machine Controller Temperature

S3Q2: T/F: You should monitor ATP and ACT values at all times.

Answer: True.

S3Q3: What is the purpose of evacuation?

To introduce reactive gases for etching.

To remove unwanted gases and contaminants.

To cool down the wafer during the etching process

Equipment: RIE

To increase the etch rate and anisotropy.

To enable high-pressure plasma generation.

Answer: B. Evacuation removes unwanted gases and creates a low-pressure environment for plasma generation.

SECTION 4) Loading the Wafer

S4Q1: In Reactive-Ion Etching (RIE), which sequence correctly describes how material is removed from a substrate?

Introduce reactive gas > Apply radio frequency (RF) power to create plasma > Ions react and sputter material > Pump out volatile byproducts.

Substrate in chamber > Introduce wet etchant > Chemical reaction occurs > Byproducts dissolve.

Substrate in chamber > Ions physically etch > Byproducts desorb > Vacuum pump removes.

Apply heat to substrate > Sublimation > Vapor removed > Surface etched

Introduce inert gas > Apply DC voltage to create plasma > Electrons bombard to remove material > Neutralize charged byproducts with counter-ions.

Answer: A

S4Q2: How do you open the RIE chamber lid?

Press one of the hoist buttons.

Turn the switch to chamber up, and it will open automatically.

Turn the switch to chamber up, and press one of the hoist buttons.

Turn the switch to chamber up, then press and hold both of the hoist buttons.

Equipment: RIE

Turn the switch to chamber up and press both of the hoist buttons.

Answer: D

SECTION 5) Etching and Running the Recipe

S5Q1: T/F: You should never change the gas concentrations before running a recipe.

Answer: False. You should always check the flow rate, process time, and power values before running a recipe and adjust them if needed.

S5Q2: Why is plasma necessary during Reactive Ion Etching? Plasma...

Acts as a heat source to melt and remove the material.

Provides UV radiation to solidify the patterned mask.

Generates reactive ions and radicals that chemically react with the material and physically sputter it away.

Creates a vacuum that draws material away from the substrate.

Forms a protective layer to prevent etching of the substrate.

Answer: C. Ions bombard the surface, physically removing the material and enchancing the vertical etch directionality (anisotropy).

S5Q3: Which is NOT a key parameter in a standard RIE process?

RF power

Evacuation duration

Chamber pressure

Gas type and its corresponding flow rate

Etch time

Answer: B, Evacuation Duration.

SECTION 6) Removing the Wafer

Equipment: RIE

S6Q1: T/F: It is impossible to see a difference in the sample’s appearance after etching without the use of a microscope.

Answer: False

S6Q2: You must wait for the turbo pump to reach 0 RPM before exiting service mode on the computer.

Answer: True

S6Q3: After using profilometry, ellipsometry, or SEM to measure thickness before and after etching, how is etch rate calculated?

Etch Rate = Time / (Initial + Final Thickness)

Etch Rate = (Final – Initial Thickness) / Time

Etch Rate = (Initial – Final Thickness) / Time

Etch Rate = (Initial + Final Thickness) / Time

Etch Rate = Time / (Initial – Final Thickness)

Answer: C, Etch Rate = (Initial – Final Thickness) / Time

SECTION 7) Front Room Machine Shutdown

S7Q1: Which of the following does NOT cause non-uniform etching across the wafer?

Poor gas distribution

Inconsistent electrode contact or RF power distribution

Chamber loading effects

Excessive etching time, leading to over-etching

Stable chamber pressure

Answer: E. Maintaining a stable and optimized chamber pressure is crucial for uniform etching.

S7Q2: How can a small area be etched on a wafer using the RIE system?

By submerging the wafer in a liquid chemical.

Equipment: RIE

By applying a broad plasma etching process to the entire wafer.

By creating a mask that exposes only the specific area to etch.

By using a laser to burn away unwanted material.

By physically scraping away the unwanted material.

Answer: C. By creating a mask that exposes only the specific area to etch.

S7Q3 T/F: The small chiller automatically turns off when you press the red power “OFF” button on the RIE machine.

Answer: True

SECTION 8) Support Room Machine Shutdown

S8Q1: T/F: When shutting down the support room machine, you turn them off in the exact same order that you turned them on.

Answer: False. You turn them off in the exact opposite direction.

S8Q2: T/F: In commercial settings, it is likely that gases will remain on all day.

Answer: True

S8Q3: How frequently should the chamber of the RIE be cleaned and maintained?

After every single etching process, regardless of type.

Only when noticeable malfunctions or performance drops occur.

Weekly or following heavy polymer-forming processes.

Monthly, using only dummy wafers for all cleaning steps.

Yearly, with only visual inspection of the chamber components.

Equipment: RIE

Answer: C. Weekly or after heavy polymer-forming processes. Dummy wafers or O2 plasma should be used to clean. Electrodes and gaskets should be inspected regularly
